title: State / Local Politician
setting: State Parliament or Council Chambers + Local Community
companySize: State Parliament (varies: NSW has 93 lower house, 42 upper house) or Local Council (varies: 5-15 councillors)
description: State and local politicians deal with the issues that most directly affect people's daily lives — hospitals, schools, roads, public transport, planning, policing, and local services. State MPs balance party politics with constituent service. Councillors are often part-time, combining political duties with other work. Both roles are deeply embedded in their communities in a way federal politicians often are not.
salary: $170,000-$190,000 AUD (state MP varies by state) / $25,000-$70,000 AUD (councillor, part-time role)

schedule.activity: Check emails and local news — local issues often break overnight via council notifications or community Facebook groups
Electorate office opens — staff begin handling constituent enquiries: planning disputes, school enrolments, hospital wait times
Site visit — inspect a road project, visit a new school building, or meet with a community group about a local issue
schedule.note: State and local politicians are expected to be visible in their community. Showing up matters.

schedule.activity: Meeting with a state government department — follow up on a constituent's casework or a local infrastructure project
Community lunch — Rotary, RSL, school P&C, or business chamber. These are where you hear what people actually care about
schedule.note: For local politicians, community lunches are not optional social events — they are intelligence gathering.

schedule.activity: Parliament sitting (state MP) or council meeting preparation (councillor) — review agenda, draft questions, prepare speeches
Media interview with local newspaper or radio about a community issue — new development approval, hospital funding, school safety
Community event — school open night, sporting club presentation, fundraising dinner, or public meeting on a contentious issue
schedule.note: Evenings are when communities come together. A local politician's diary is full of evening commitments.

schedule.activity: Respond to constituent emails and social media messages, prepare for tomorrow's meetings
Family time — though the phone is always on. A constituent crisis can call at any hour
schedule.note: Local politicians are accessible in a way federal politicians are not. Your phone number is known.

responsibilities: Represent your community on issues that directly affect daily life — health, education, transport, planning
Handle constituent casework — often more hands-on than federal MPs
Attend parliament sitting weeks (state) or monthly council meetings (local)
Engage with local media, schools, sporting clubs, and community organisations
Advocate for local infrastructure and services funding
Participate in committees and inquiries at state or local level
Be visible and accessible in your community — people expect to see you at local events
challenges: State/local issues get less media attention but affect people more directly
Councillors are often part-time with limited staff support
Planning and development decisions create intense local conflict
Constituent expectations often exceed what the role can deliver
State politicians are caught between local needs and party discipline
rewards: Tangible, visible impact — the road gets fixed, the school gets built, the park gets upgraded
Deep community connection — you know your constituents personally
Entry point for a political career — many federal politicians started in local or state government
Hands-on governance experience that federal roles often lack
The satisfaction of solving problems for real people in your community
careerPath: State MP → Minister → Premier (or transition to federal politics)
